titlestmt.titleproper: Correctional Association of New York; Records
Correctional Association of New York; Records apap014
titlestmt.author: Cynthia A. Morrison (1990), Bonita L. Weddle (2000), Rosann Santos (2000), Liam Sperrazza and Milo Reynoso (2026)

publicationstmt.publisher: M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections & Archives

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-06-24 01:00:02 -0400.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

change.item: Encoded in EAD by Conversion and encoding by ArchProteus
Reprocessing of the original materials accessioned in 1989, processing and arrangement of materials accessioned in 2006, 2017, 2024, and 2025.

repository.corpname: M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections & Archives

did.unittitle: Correctional Association of New York Records
did.abstract: The Correctional Association of New York (CANY) Records includes materials that pertain to the operation of the organization concerning the incarceration of individuals in New York State prisons and correctional facilities. CANY has the legislative ability to enter the correctional facilities and jails in New York to meet and speak with incarcerated individuals, and create reports of conditions. Records include the materials that relate to the operation and administration of the organization, research materials, committee files, and materials that concern the correctional facilities and jails in New York. While the organization was founded in 1844, there is a limited quantity of records from the 19th century.
did.physloc: The materials are located onsite in the department.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Processed in 1990 by Cynthia A. Morrison with revisions made in 2000 by Bonita L. Weddle and Rosann Santos. In 2026, the 1989 materials were re-processed and inter-filed, and the materials accessioned in 2006, 2017, 2024 and 2025 were processed and arranged by Liam Sperrazza.

bioghist.head: Administrative History
bioghist.p: The Correctional Association of New York was founded in 1844 by John W. Edmonds, President of the Board of Inspectors at Sing Sing Prison. Originally named the Prison Association of New York, the organization was formed to ameliorate the conditions of criminal defendants and incarcerated individuals, improve the discipline and administration of local jails and state prisons, and furnish assistance and encouragement to incarcerated individuals after discharge. It is the only private organization in the state that has the power to conduct on-site examinations of state and local correctional facilities and report its findings and recommendations to governmental authorities.

Since 1846, it has been charged with submitting an annual report on prison conditions in New York State to the New York State Assembly. The Association has always furnished aid to male and female prisoners. However, roughly a decade after its foundation a number of members became convinced that the special needs of female incarcerated individuals were not being addressed. Led by Issac G. Hopper, the chair of the Association's Female Department, they left the Association and formed their own group, the Women's Prison Association of New York.

During the nineteenth century, the organization took a leading role in prison reform in the United States and the world. It formed the National Prison Association (now the American Prison Association), a nationwide umbrella organization, in 1870. Two years later, it started the International Prison Congress, a world-wide gathering of prison officials and social reformers that met every five years. The League of Nations eventually assumed responsibility for running the conferences, which are now held under the auspices of the United Nations.

The Association was active in promoting numerous criminal justice system reforms in New York State. The organization was instrumental in creating New York State's first probation and parole systems (c.1875) and pressed for the creation of separate facilities for youthful offenders (1864-74) and a separate juvenile justice system (1874-1904).

The Association sought to ensure that incarcerated individuals had opportunities to acquire formal education and advocated the creation of prison libaries (1904-24). It also fought to ensure that incarcerated individuals could learn a trade while incarcerated and that their labor was not exploited. Other policies the Association advocated included: a prison classification system; opposition to corporal punishment (1844-64); the Presidential Commutation Act; special care facilities for inmates afflicted with tuberculosis; establishment of a psychiatric clinic at Sing Sing Prison; creation of the New York City Parole Commission; establishment of prison libraries (1904-24); development of policies which enabled prisoners to contribute to the war effort by working in war-related industries (1940-42); establishment of a Crime Victims Compensation Board (1965-66); creation of an adequate public defender system.

In the 1960s and 1970s, the organization advocated the establishment of a Crime Victims Compensation Board (1965-66) and pressed for the decriminalization of pornography, sexual acts between consenting adults, and other crimes it deemed "victimless." In the early 1970s, the Association's legislative mandate was revised so that the organization would no longer be required to present annual reports to the New York Assembly, consolidating the management of correctional facilities to the New York State Department of Correctional Services, or DOCS (now, the New York State Department of Corrections and Community Services, or DOCCS), primarily due to the drastic increase of incarcerated individuals in the state during the 1970s due to the Rockefeller Drug Laws. This shifting of correctional oversight led to the Association becoming more of a public education and lobbying organization, while still enabling them the ability to access correctional facilities and speak with incarcerated individuals.

Activities of the Association in the late 20th century include the AIDS in Prison Project, the Public Policy Project, the Prison Overcrowding Project, the Prison and Jail Conditions Project, and the Prison Visiting Committee (1980-present). The Association has also been active in efforts to educate the public about the immense costs incurred by city, county, and state correctional agencies and to promote alternatives to incarceration for non-violent offenders. Recent projects conducted by the Association in the 21st century have focused on incarcerated women in New York correctional facilities, as seen with the Women in Prison Project, healthcare in correctional facilities, and solitary confinement. In 2021, the Association's position as a monitoring organization of the New York State correctional facilities was codified into state law.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Correctional Association of New York (CANY) Records documents the activities of the organization as they fulfilled their organization goals to ensure the wellbeing of incarcerated individuals in New York State correctional facilities and prisons as provided by their legislative mandate. Included with the collection are materials concerning the daily operation of the organization, and materials for the longevity of the organization.

The collection includes minutes and agendas, administrative materials (i.e., committee materials, board of directors mailings, etc.), correspondence (both internal and external), fundraising materials, audio-visual materials, surveys and statistics, position papers, research papers (i.e., AIDS, incarcerated women, mental health, etc.), project and program files and reports (i.e., wellbeing of incarcerated individuals, social services, correctional facility inadequacies, etc.) , organization history materials, event planning materials (i.e., The 1844 Medal Award), legislative lobbying materials, operational materials, and general reference files. While the organization was founded in 1844, records are predominantly from the twentieth century.

arrangement.head: Arrangement of the Collection
arrangement.p: Each of the series is arranged alphabetically, excluding Series 2, which is arranged chronologically. The collection is organized as follows:

list.item: Series 1: Administration and Board of Directors Files, 1867-2017
Series 2: Annual Reports to the New York State Legislature, 1844-1979
Series 5: Early Program and Bureau Files, 1967-1983

acqinfo.head: Acquisition Information
acqinfo.p: All items in this manuscript group were donated to the University Libraries, M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives, by the Correctional Association of New York in 1990. Additional accessions were received in 2006, 2017, 2024 and 2025.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Related collections in the M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives can be found in the subject guide for Criminal Justice and Prisons.

accessrestrict.head: Access
accessrestrict.p: Access to portions of Series 1, 5, 6, 7, and 8, is restricted as they contain personal identifiable information related to incarcerated individuals and others, and correspondence with incarcerated individuals. Restricted folders in these series are noted at the file-level, and requests to view these materials must be discussed with a staff member. Series 3 is completely restricted due to a large quantity of personal identifiable information of incarcerated individuals, and correspondence with incarcerated individuals throughout the series. Requests to view Series 3 must be discussed with a staff member. Consult a staff member for further details.

userestrict.head: Copyright
userestrict.p: The researcher assumes full responsibility for conforming with the laws of copyright. Whenever possible, the M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives will provide information about copyright owners and other restrictions, but the legal determination ultimately rests with the researcher. Requests for permission to publish material from this collection should be discussed with the Head of Special Collections and Archives.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Preferred citation for this material is as follows:

Identification of specific item, series, box, folder, Correctional Association of New York Records 1844-1988 (APAP-014). M. E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives, University Libraries, University at Albany, State University of New York (hereafter referred to as the Correctional Association of New York Records).

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: The archivist transferred two monographs: Sex and Race, Volume I by J. A. Rogers and My Sister's Keeper: A Book for Women Returning Home from Prison or Jail by the Correctional Association of New York to the book division of Special Collections. It will be catalogued and included in the University Library Catalog.

physdesc.physfacet: 11 reels of 35 mm microfilm and bound copies

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists exclusively of the annual reports on prison and jail conditions that the Prison Association/Correctional Association submitted to the New York State Assembly. The reports contain minutes of meetings, presidential addresses, committee reports, reports on individual prisons and prison conditions, prison reform campaigns in New York State, transcripts of testimony before the NYS legislature on pending policy measures, and lists of members and corresponding members of the Association. Reports published during the latter half of the nineteenth century chronicle the Association's role in establishing national and international prison reform organizations and conferences and furnish information about prison policies elsewhere in the United States and the world. There are a select range of physical copies available upon request of Annual Reports to the New York State Assembly ranging from 1844 to 1969. However, the bulk of these reports are from the 20th century.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged chronologically. The microfilm versions are housed in the University Library Microfilm Collection, call number HV 8987 P684. Bound copies of the 1867-1878 and 1921-1969 Annual Reports are also available in the M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: The Annual Reports are available on microfilm in the University Library's Microfilm Collection, call number HV 8987 P684.
Bound copies of the 1867-1878 and 1921-1969 Annual Reports are also available in the M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives.

did.unittitle: 1st Annual Report appendix, First Report of the Female Department of the Prison Association of New York
3rd Annual Report Part 2 Report of the Prison Discipline Committee; Folded Plates
5th Annual Report; Page 364 is misnumbered and 464; Page 395-396 are mutilated; Page 397-398; and pp. 399-400 are stubs
10th Annual Report; Page 89 is misnumbered as Page 8
15th-16th Annual Report; Issue Combined; Tape Damage
24th Annual Report 14 unnumbered plates; 3 unnumbered folded plates.
57th Annual Report; 8 unnumbered plates; 1 unnumbered folded plate
70th Annual Report 30 unnumbered plates. 1 unumbered folded plate
